data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2045 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio delivered a gain and outperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation was the primary factor in the fund's relative strength.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to results, primarily as a result of our decision to maintain an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. An underweight in international stocks also made a small contribution. On the other hand, an overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. An overweight in U.S. mid caps, which didn't keep pace with large caps, had a modest, negative effect on results. The fund's absolute return portfolio further detracted, as did underlying manager performance.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

data.title: AAON: A Secular Growth Play Facing Current Challenges
data.description:
Shares of AAON, Inc. (NASDAQ:AAON) have become part of the momentum ride, which data center suppliers have benefited from in recent years. Shares, however, are down substantially from the peak, in part as the business still has large legacy HVAC operations but also as the business suffers from execution issues.

In between higher expectations amidst the sound positioning and some near-term execution issues, valuations are very demanding, and while the company will likely see spectacular profit growth in the near term, I fear that resulting performance will not provide a very compelling valuation at current levels amidst too much ambiguity out there today.

Not Your Typical HVAC Company

The paragraph header is how AAON describes its own business, claiming to be an entrepreneurial disruptor in the HVAC industry, driven by a focus on providing customer value, agility, and innovative practices.

Founded back in 1988, AAON operates two separate brand families, which each cater to a distinctive business line and applications. The namesake AAON brand focuses on the production and sale of rooftop units, handlers, and condensing units used in education, retail, manufacturing, and office applications.

BASX provides air handling, liquid cooling, and cleanroom environments for datacenters and their related cleanrooms. The business is mostly focused on the US, and the AAON namesake brand makes up the vast majority of sales, about three-quarters of total revenues, with BASX being the smaller unit, yet one seeing a 40% compounded annual growth rate over the past three years, while AAON is a much more stable and traditional business.

This creates an interesting dynamic, namely that of a growth gem in the business line, as this business becomes larger and thereby boosts the pace of organic growth of the entire business. This is clearly visible in the long-term performance of the business. Over the past decade, revenues have risen from just over $300 million to nearly $1.3 billion, all amidst a stable share count. All this has been reflected in the share price, as a $15 stock a decade ago now exchanges hands at levels in the mid-eighties.

Valuation Thoughts

Earlier this year, the company reported results for 2024, which really require an in-depth look to fully comprehend the trends taking place. Full-year sales were reported up nearly 3% to $1.20 billion, as GAAP operating profits were down 8% to $209 million, with GAAP earnings down 5% to $2.02 per share.

The company really suffered from struggles in the core AAON business, offset by strong growth in the BASX segment, with the latter being the driver behind a 70% increase in the year backlog to $867 million.

The company operated with about $150 million in net debt, no issue at all in light of a $272 million EBITDA number reported for the year, with these net debt levels being the result of a $213 million capital expenditure spent. This compares to just a $62 million combined depreciation and amortization charge incurred last year, indicating very large net capital expenditures being made, geared towards capacity expansion at BASX.

Needless to say, valuations were creeping up mostly because of fantasy around BASX as shares doubled to a high of $140 in 2024, which, given the earnings power just discussed, results in sky-high valuations. In all fairness, shares have fallen and have been trading in a $60-$100 range since the spring of the year.

Despite the uneven results for 2024, the company announced a $30 million buyback program in March and announced a 25% increase in the dividend, although the percentage hike is more impressive than the actual dividend, now payable at $0.40 per share each year.

Growth Comes Through

The company reported very interesting first quarter results, with total sales up 23% to $322 million, but amidst severe margin pressure, operating profits were down a quarter to $35 million, with adjusted earnings down 9 cents to $0.35 per share.

The interesting part is that while AAON segment sales were down 19% to $189 million, it were BASX sales, which were up no less than 375% to $132 million, now making up 40% of sales here. This momentum furthermore drove a huge increase in the backlog to $1.03 billion, up $160 million in the first quarter.

In Augusts, second quarter sales were posted flat at $311 million and change as the backlog rose further to $1.12 billion, driven by continued order intake at BASX. Results were impacted by ERP implementation issues and resulting supply chain disruptions. The reporting was a bit hard to read as BASX sales rose 20% to $68 million, but the reporting nature differs from the first quarter as the size of this segment is much different from first quarter numbers, but this might have to do with my unfamiliarity with the business.

Similar to the first quarter, company-wide margins continued to come down, with quarterly operating income reported at $23 million. As a result, earnings in the first half of the year are cut in half to $0.54 per share, which makes that earnings only trend around a dollar per share here, which is not really fair either as some of the current problems are really of short-term nature.

Due to net investments made into the business, net debt has risen to $317 million, something to take note of as EBITDA fell to $103 million for the first half of the year, driven by headwinds in margins. This results in a roughly 1.5 times leverage ratio, no issue, despite continued elevated capital expenditure needs.

What Now?

Right now, the 83 million shares of AAON trade at $85, granting equity a $7.0 billion valuation and a modestly higher enterprise valuation. This is applied to a business that generates about $1.3 billion in sales, mostly a struggling AAON namesake business and a promising BASX segment.

The issue is that earnings have taken a beating and are very modest in relation to the current valuations here, as profits around $2 per share in 2024 are set to come down meaningfully this year, only trending just over a dollar per share here. Even if current woes are accounted for and 2024 earnings return, this yields very demanding valuation multiples, too demanding to see appeal despite the promise around its positioning.

These near-term headwinds relate to challenged implementation of ERP systems, supply chain issues, and large investment requirements, while the long-term outlook remains good. This sound positioning does not rhyme with near-term margins, as the picture is a bit messy from this perspective, although expected to clear soon. Full-year sales are seen up double digits to around $1.35 billion, up around $150 million in dollar terms, as revenues year to date are up $58 million here.

So the question is what earnings will look like once the issues are resolved. A 15-20% operating margin target on $1.5 billion sales looks quite reasonable, which would translate into earnings around $2.50 per share at the high end of the range, still creating a demanding set-up here.

Amidst these hiccups and the foregoing discussion, I am cautious here because even if growth returns in the near term and margins recover, current earnings power is relatively modest in relation to the valuation here.

However, the positioning is very interesting to keep track of from here going forward, but unwilling to participate here or have conviction on the growth story.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2030 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio delivered a gain and outperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation was the primary factor in the fund's relative strength.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to results, primarily as a result of our decision to maintain an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. An overweight in emergingmarket debt contributed, as well. On the other hand, the real assets portfolio detracted due to negative returns for energy stocks. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms—also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. The fund's absolute return portfolio further detracted, as did underlying manager performance.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. data.title: RISR: Unique Income ETF, Negative Duration, 5.6% Dividend Yield
data.description:
I've covered the FolioBeyond Alternative Income and Interest Rate Hedge ETF (NYSEARCA:RISR) several times in the past. I've generally focused on the fund's negative duration, an unusual characteristic, and one that makes the fund an effective interest rate hedge at the portfolio level and an effective trading vehicle for more hawkish investors. RISR is also a positive carry investment, meaning it should deliver positive returns to investors long-term/when rates are flat. RISR is a fantastic ETF and a buy, although I would focus on using the fund as a rate hedge and think of it as such.

RISR - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

RISR has a fantastic pitchbook explaining its strategy and investment thesis in a simple manner. I'll explain the fund in my own words, but I do recommend reading through the pitchbook.

RISR invests in MBS. Simplifying things a bit, MBS are diversified portfolios of mortgages, so investing in these means investing in mortgages and receiving mortgage payments as dividends.

RISR invests in agency mortgages, the highest-quality, highest-rated mortgages available, with smaller investments in cash and cash alternatives.

RISR focuses on interest-only MBS. Investors in these securities only receive the interest rate portion of any applicable mortgage payment without any principal payment. These payments are positively correlated to rates. Lower rates increase mortgage refinancing, meaning lower mortgage interest rate payments, meaning lower payments on interest-only MBS. Higher rates decrease refinancing and hence have a broadly opposite effect. The impact here is significant enough for interest-only MBS to have negative duration, meaning their prices decrease as rates go down, and vice versa. Duration currently stands at -4.0.

Do note that the vast majority of investments don't function in the same way. Traditional bonds or treasuries have fixed rates from issuance until maturity, so lower interest rates do not (immediately) decrease the interest paid on existing bonds, only new ones. Rates going down thus increase demand for older, higher-yielding bonds or treasuries, boosting their price. Exact opposite scenario compared to interest-only MBS.

As an example of the above, RISR's share price skyrocketed in early 2022, the start of the most recent Fed hiking cycle. More traditional MBS, treasuries, and bonds saw declining prices instead. Different impact, as these are different securities with different characteristics.

RISR's negative duration is the fund's most important characteristic and what differentiates it from its peers. RISR's negative duration means that the fund can be used as a short-term trading vehicle for hawkish investors or to hedge an investor's rate risk or exposure at the portfolio level. Let's have a look at these two alternatives.

Short-Term Hawkish Trade

RISR's negative duration means that the fund can be used as a short-term trading vehicle for hawkish investors. Significant increases in rates would lead to significant gains and outperformance for the fund, as was the case during 2022:

Do bear in mind that the 2022 inflation surge/hiking cycle was incredibly aggressive and unusual. Inflation rarely goes up so much, and so the Fed is rarely forced into hiking rates so aggressively. I would expect any future hiking cycle to be less aggressive, with RISR seeing smaller gains in turn.

I'm not expecting any short-term increase in rates, so I would not be using RISR for these purposes.

Interest Rate Hedge

RISR's negative duration means that the fund can be used to hedge an investor's overall rate risk or exposure.

Investors with sizable investments in treasuries, high-quality bonds, or other securities with high duration could pair these investments with RISR, reducing or canceling out their rate exposure at the portfolio level. As an example, a small investment in RISR could have canceled out any losses from a significant investment in more traditional MBS during 2022 (see above). I would expect the same during any future hiking cycle, and for treasuries and high-quality bonds.

As previously mentioned, I'm not expecting any short-term increase in rates, so I don't believe that such a hedge should prove profitable. Nevertheless, such a hedge would reduce portfolio risk and volatility and should prove to be a long-term positive. At the same time, some investors might prefer not to make any sort of rate or macro forecast for these to influence their investment decisions/portfolio returns. Using RISR as a rate hedge makes perfect sense for these investors.

Positive Carry

RISR's MBS are positive carry instruments, meaning they produce a positive amount of income with positive expected returns. RISR's negative duration is its key differentiator, but it also has a solid 5.7% SEC yield and should see perfectly adequate long-returns.

Although the above seems a bit banal, I do think it has several important implications for investors.

RISR's positive carry should ensure outperformance relative to more traditional short-treasury/bond ETFs or strategies. These are quite popular in trading circles for hawkish traders, and so the comparison comes naturally. RISR has outperformed the ProShares Short 20+ Year Treasury ETF (TBF) since inception, for instance.

In my opinion, the difference in performance between RISR and TBF should be much starker long-term, or when rates are flat.

RISR's positive carry should ensure positive long-term returns, barring interest rates consistently declining from here on out. More traditional short-treasury/bond ETFs should see negative returns instead. TBF is down more than 40% since inception in late 2009, for instance. Do note that the graph above starts from RISR's inception and the graph below since TBF's creation.

RISR's positive carry should ensure positive returns at the end of the rate cycle, same as most other bonds and fixed-income securities. RISR's negative duration should decrease portfolio risk and volatility throughout. Investing in RISR should thus decrease risk while keeping returns constant, long-term at least.

Ideally, I would show the above by looking into the performance of RISR and a few other bond index ETFs during a rate cycle, from start to finish. RISR is too young for that. Luckily for us, rates have remained reasonably flat these past six months, with benchmark 10-year treasury and 30-year mortgages both marginally down.

And with RISR seeing perfectly adequate, perhaps slightly below-average, returns:

RISR should see particularly strong returns if rates increase, but returns should prove perfectly adequate if rates are flat or marginally down. The former differentiates RISR from its peers, but the latter is still important.

As an aside, that last point explains why I'm both bullish on RISR and on the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (TLTW). TLTW is very different from RISR, with very different exposure to rates. RISR's share price increases when rates are up. TLTW's share price increases when rates are down, although the gains here are somewhat limited (the massive 17.1% distribution makes up the difference). Although the rate exposure is very different, both ETFs are up YTD.

RISR and TLTW, for that matter, are not only plays on rates, and that matters. It is perfectly possible, and I think likely, for both ETFs to see positive returns moving forward, even though their exposure to rates is very different. It is reasonable to be bullish on both, especially if one does not have strong opinions on interest rate policy/trajectory in the short term.

Conclusion

RISR invests in interest-only MBS securities with negative duration. RISR's negative duration makes the fund an effective interest rate hedge, or hawkish trade. RISR's positive carry and good 5.7% SEC yield make it a reasonable income vehicle too, although I think the negative duration is the more important or relevant characteristic.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2020 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio, while also delivering a gain, didn't keep pace with its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation and underlying manager results each weighed on relative performance.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute termsalso weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight the emerging markets. The fund also benefited from having an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. On the other hand, the fund's absolute return portfolio detracted. Underlying manager performance also detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the bond portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. data.title: RH: A Comfortable Prospect On A Turnaround
data.description:
I have a good track record in investing, but nobody has a perfect track record. Every investor, if they are in the game long enough, makes calls that don't work out. A good example of one of mine that fits this description would be RH (NYSE:RH). The business, which operates in the luxury retail home furnishings market, has seen a meaningful decline in share price, with the stock falling 34.3% since I rated it a ‘buy’ in March of 2022. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 41.2%. As disappointing as the returns have been, I do believe that the situation is improving. To be clear, there are headwinds for the company moving forward. But based on recent financial performance and how the stock is valued on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies, I do believe that it justifies a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

This play makes sense this time

Operationally speaking, RH serves as a retailer and luxury lifestyle brand business that caters to the home furnishings market. It considers itself a fully integrated player in this space, with sales generated through its galleries, which serve as showrooms for its brand, as well as through its websites and other sources. The firm operates retail locations throughout the US, Canada, the UK, Germany, Belgium, and Spain. And it even has several Design Gallery locations that operate as large format stores that include in them, among other things, on-site restaurants, rooftop cafes, and wine bars.

Over the last few years now, RH has experienced a bumpy ride. From 2022 to 2023, for instance, revenue for the business plummeted from $3.59 billion to $3.03 billion. This was in spite of the fact that the number of retail locations had increased from 67 to 70 while the number of outlets grew from 37 to 42. This drop in revenue was caused by a return to more normal spending patterns after the COVID-19 pandemic caused a surge in home spending. During that time, government stimulus made spending easier, plus individuals were spending a lot more time at home because of social distancing. That caused major investments to be made in the types of goods that RH offers. In addition to this return to more normal levels, the company also saw pain because of high interest rates that made costly purchases prohibitive.

While that drop in revenue was painful, it is important to point out that, in 2024, revenue staged a slight recovery, growing to $3.18 billion. This was caused by the introduction of new collections that appealed to customers, a surge in catalog circulation, and increased hospitality revenue thanks to new gallery openings. Outlet revenue for the company also improved during this time. The year-over-year increase for the business would have been even better had it not been for the fact that, in 2023, the company had an extra operating week that added $50 million in revenue during that year.

Even though revenue has started to recover, as of 2024, the same cannot be said of profits and cash flows. From 2022 to 2023, net income for the company plummeted from $528.6 million to $127.6 million. There was a further drop to $72.4 million last year. This was in spite of the fact that, from 2023 to 2024, the overall gross profit for the company grew from $1.39 billion to $1.41 billion. However, selling, general, and administrative costs popped from $1.02 billion to $1.09 billion. Interest expense grew also, climbing from $198.3 million to $230.6 million. Other profitability metrics worsened during this time. Operating cash flow was only $17.1 million last year. That was down from the $202.2 million the previous year and the $403.7 million a year before that. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline over the last three years from $902.1 million to $431.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $930.5 million to $539.1 million.

One thing that I am encouraged is that, so far, 2025 is looking up. We only have data for the first quarter of the year. But during that time, revenue of $814 million easily surpassed the $727 million reported last year. This increase in sales for the business was caused by core operations and management attributed to continued product transformation and platform changes. The firm also benefited from new gallery openings and an increase in outlet revenue.

Profitability for the company is also getting better. The firm went from a net loss of $3.6 million in the first quarter of 2024 to a profit of $8 million the same time this year. Operating cash flow jumped from $56.1 million to $86.6 million. And on an adjusted basis, the increase was from $74.2 million to $88.7 million. Lastly, EBITDA for the company grew from $89.1 million to $106.4 million.

Management has been working hard to fundamentally transform the company. For this year as a whole, the firm is forecasting revenue growth of between 10% and 13%. International momentum has been particularly appealing, with gallery revenue for its RH England operations being up 47% in the first quarter and online demand rising by 44%. In RH Munich and RH Dusseldorf, demand seems to be up around 60%. The firm is also working on opening RH Paris, with management even describing it as ‘our most elegant and inspiring gallery yet.’ Unless something changes in the timeline, that location should open early next month. Management continues to launch new brand extensions. But a significant one that was originally planned for the second half of this year has now been pushed to the spring of 2026 in order for the company to mitigate the impact of tariffs.

Speaking of tariffs, management has been a bit cautious. But the good news is that the firm should be able to adjust to the new economic order. In the first quarter of this year, 16% of what the business purchased was sourced out of China. By the end of this fiscal year, that should drop to roughly 2%. By the end of 2025, management believes that around 52% of upholstered furniture will be produced in the US, with another 21% being produced in Italy. These challenges that the company is facing have not prevented it from forecasting an EBITDA margin of 20% to 21% this year. At the midpoint with the expected revenue growth as well, this would mean EBITDA of $727.1 million based on revenue of $3.55 billion. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $582.1 million.

If we use these estimates provided by management, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These multiples are at the high end of what I would consider to be the fair value range. And relative to other similar firms, shares are a bit on the cheap side. In the table below, I compared RH to five other enterprises. What I found was that only one of these companies was cheaper than it is on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. And when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, only two of the five were cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
RH 7.6 10.4
Arhaus (ARHS) 12.4 5.7
Beyond (BYON) 54.6 28.8
Haverty Furniture Companies (HVT) 7.0 10.1
Sleep Number Corporation (SNBR) 82.8 13.5
Hooker Furnishings (HOFT) 19.0 21.2

Takeaway

At this point in time, I definitely understand that I overestimated the company and its potential a couple of years ago. Now, I am more measured. Having said that, it is clear that the company is recovering nicely. 2025 should be a solid year for the business, and it's likely that future years will be positive as well. This is especially the case thanks to its international expansion efforts. Add on top of this how the stock is currently priced and the firm's tariff mitigation strategy, and I do believe that rating it a soft ‘buy’ would be appropriate at this time.

While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.
